title: Gay Witch Hunt

jokes.text: Which means, at my ten-year high school reunion, it will not say, 'Ryan Howard is a temp.' 'Ryan Howard is a junior sales associate at a mid-range paper supply firm.'
jokes.setup: Ryan proudly announces his promotion from temp
jokes.punchline: His new title is equally unimpressive and pathetic
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Ryan's delusional pride in a marginal promotion. The specificity of 'mid-range paper supply firm' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I got promoted from temp.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's great, Ryan! What's your new title?

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Vice President of Regional Sales.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's the same title I have.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no. Ryan's is different. His has 'Vice' in front of it.

jokes.text: That'll show'em.
jokes.setup: Ryan's pathetic job title revelation
jokes.punchline: His sincere belief this will impress people
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - three words that perfectly cap the delusion. Classic Office deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I'm a temp. But I'm not just any temp — I'm a *VP of Business Development*.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Well, technically I answer to Michael, but the business cards say VP. That's the important part.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Girls love ambition. And a business card. Especially a business card that says VP.

jokes.text: You have no idea how long I've wanted to do that.
jokes.setup: Presumably Jim and Pam just kissed
jokes.punchline: Jim's admission of long-suppressed desire
jokes.explanation: More emotional than comedic but the awkwardness of the confession has comedic weight.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I have to tell you something. I'm in love with you. What do you want me to say?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm sorry I didn't say it before I kissed you. And I don't know how long I can work here and see you every day and not tell you this. But I have to.

jokes.text: I think we're just drunk. No, I'm not drunk. Are you drunk? No.
jokes.setup: Post-kiss awkwardness
jokes.punchline: The immediate rationalization and denial
jokes.explanation: Classic post-kiss awkwardness, realistic but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That was... that shouldn't have happened.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yeah, no, totally. We were just... I mean, we weren't thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Right. It was just a moment. A weird moment.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Exactly. And it doesn't mean anything.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Nothing at all.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We're just... we're friends. Coworkers.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Best friends, even.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: So this changes nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Absolutely nothing.

jokes.text: You're really gonna marry him?
jokes.setup: Jim processing what just happened
jokes.punchline: His disbelief that she'd still marry Roy after their kiss
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jim's hope and confusion, economically delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: She's still gonna marry Roy? After that kiss?

jokes.text: Jim is gone. He's gone. I miss him so much. Oh, I cry myself to sleep. Jim!
jokes.setup: Dwight talking about missing Jim
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top emotional display
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - his dramatic overwrought emotion is completely in character and unexpected.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I miss Jim. Every morning I wake up and I think about him. The way he used to sit at that desk, the way he would look at me with those eyes... I can't eat. I can't sleep. I just lie awake at night thinking about Jim's stupid face.

jokes.text: False. I do not miss him.
jokes.setup: Dwight's emotional outburst about missing Jim
jokes.punchline: Immediate contradiction with his signature 'False'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal - peak Dwight logic. Perfect economy and character integration with his signature catchphrase.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I miss Jim. He's my best friend and I don't know what I'd do without him.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: False.

jokes.text: No, that is the fun of this place. I call everybody 'faggy.' Why would anyone find that offensive?
jokes.setup: Michael defending his use of offensive language
jokes.punchline: His complete obliviousness to why it's offensive
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael ignorance - the comedy comes from his genuine confusion about why this would be problematic.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't see what the big deal is. I use that word all the time at home. My kids love it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's just a word. Words don't hurt people. Sticks and stones may break my bones, but words will never hurt me.

jokes.text: It wasn't just an action movie, it was 'Die Hard.'
jokes.setup: Defending his calling Oscar faggy for preferring Shakespeare in Love
jokes.punchline: His reverence for Die Hard as the ultimate masculine film
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Michael's masculine insecurity expressed through action movie worship.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Look, Shakespeare in Love? That's not a real movie. Die Hard is a real movie. It's got everything - action, explosions, a guy in a tank top fighting terrorists. That's cinema. That's what separates the men from the boys.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Michael, that doesn't even make sense.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It makes perfect sense. Die Hard is the greatest movie ever made. It's got Reginald VelJohnson.

jokes.text: You don't... you don't call retarded people retards. It's bad taste. You call your friends retards when they're acting retarded.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to explain his offensive language use
jokes.punchline: His completely backwards logic about appropriate language
jokes.explanation: Peak Michael logic - his attempt at sensitivity reveals even deeper insensitivity. Quotable in its awfulness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not racist. I'm colorblind. I don't see color.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So when I use the word that I shouldn't use, it doesn't matter because I don't see race anyway.

jokes.text: I have been calling people faggy since I was in junior high, and I have never made this mistake.
jokes.setup: Michael apologizing to Oscar
jokes.punchline: Framing accidentally calling a gay person faggy as a 'mistake'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael logic - he's not sorry for using the slur, just for using it on an actual gay person.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oscar, I want to apologize for what I said. It was a mistake.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: A mistake? You don't just accidentally say that word.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You're right, you're right. I'm sorry. I feel terrible.

jokes.text: You know? I'm just- I-I can't even imagine- the... thing.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to express how straight he is
jokes.punchline: His inability to even say 'gay sex'
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Michael's discomfort with homosexuality made visible through his verbal fumbling.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not homophobic, I'm very straight. I have no problem with gay people at all. I just... I can't even say the words.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Gay... gay sex. No, I can't do it.

jokes.text: Maybe we could go out for a beer sometime, and you could tell me... how you do that to another dude.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to be accepting of Oscar
jokes.punchline: His creepy curiosity about gay sex mechanics
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael - his attempt at acceptance becomes deeply inappropriate. Perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oscar, I just want you to know that I'm totally cool with you being gay. I mean, I've watched Will & Grace.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So, uh, I have some questions. Like, logistically speaking, how does that even... I mean, where does...

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Michael, no.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to be a good ally! That's what allies do, right? Ask detailed questions about the mechanics?

jokes.text: That sounds like a great, wonderful idea. Let's do that.
jokes.setup: Michael's inappropriate suggestion about discussing gay sex
jokes.punchline: Oscar's sarcastic enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Good reaction comedy - Oscar's deadpan sarcasm as defense mechanism.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Oh, that sounds like a great use of company time.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm gay.
jokes.setup: Oscar's internal struggle about being outed
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact admission after the buildup
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - the anticlimatic delivery after his internal monologue.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: I'm gay.

jokes.text: Hey, Big Tuna. You're single, right?
jokes.setup: Andy addressing Jim with a nickname
jokes.punchline: The absurd 'Big Tuna' nickname itself
jokes.explanation: Great nickname comedy - 'Big Tuna' becomes instantly memorable and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Big Tuna.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why do you call me that?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Because you're big and you're a tuna.

jokes.text: She's completely crazy.
jokes.setup: Andy asking if Jim thinks a woman is hot
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting himself with warning
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up attraction then immediately warns against it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Do you think she's hot?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, she's attractive.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, don't get any ideas. She's way out of your league.

jokes.text: Steer clear, Big Tuna. Head for open waters.
jokes.setup: Warning Jim about the crazy woman
jokes.punchline: Extending the tuna metaphor with nautical advice
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay - extending the tuna metaphor into nautical territory. Well-crafted and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Dude, that woman is crazy. She's like a tuna.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A tuna?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, you gotta throw her back in the water before she drowns.

jokes.text: I ate a tuna sandwich on my first day... so Andy started calling me Big Tuna. I don't think any of them actually know my real name.
jokes.setup: Explaining the Big Tuna nickname origin
jokes.punchline: The realization that they don't know his actual name
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about workplace dynamics and how nicknames stick.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It started because I caught a really big tuna.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Wait, what's your real name again?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: ...Jim. It's Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Right, right. Big Tuna.

jokes.text: I went to Cornell. You ever heard of it? I graduated in four years, I never studied once, I was drunk the whole time, and I sang in the a capella group, 'Here comes Treble.'
jokes.setup: Andy bragging about his college experience
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous a capella group name 'Here comes Treble'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character establishment - the pun 'Here comes Treble' is perfectly Andy, combining arrogance with corniness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was in an a capella group in college called Here Comes Treble.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We were pretty much the best group on campus.

jokes.text: He's always looking at the camera like this. What is that?
jokes.setup: Andy commenting on Jim's behavior
jokes.punchline: Breaking the fourth wall about Jim's signature camera looks
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor - acknowledging the documentary format through a character who doesn't understand it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Jim does this thing where he just looks at the camera like it's going to change something.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: *looks at camera*

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Like that. See? Nothing changed. Your problems are still there, Jim.

jokes.text: Well, he's not dressed in women's clothes, so...
jokes.setup: Asked if she can tell Oscar is gay
jokes.punchline: Her simplistic understanding of gay stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Phyllis's naive understanding of sexuality.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Well, Oscar's always talking about interior decorating, and he's very well-groomed. Plus he cried during that movie where the dog dies.

jokes.text: Hey, what about Angela? She's hard and severe. She could be a gay woman. I don't know. I can imagine her with another woman. Can't you?
jokes.setup: Michael trying to identify other gay employees
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate speculation about Angela's sexuality
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriateness - his crude stereotyping and oversharing.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So Angela, I've been thinking... you know, Oscar's gay, right? And I'm wondering if maybe you're... you know... I mean, you're very... structured. Very organized. That's a gay thing, right?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Michael, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, the way you organize those cat figurines, and you always wear those sensible shoes...

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: I'm not gay, Michael. I'm a Christian woman with a cat.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, okay. I'm just trying to be an ally here.

jokes.text: Let's call him and get the website. What's gaydar? Oh, oh, gaydar, yes! No, uh, I think they have it at Sharper Image.
jokes.setup: Dwight doesn't know what gaydar is
jokes.punchline: Michael pretending it's an actual device sold in stores
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist humor - treating a metaphorical concept as a literal product. Well-structured misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What is gaydar?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's this device you can buy. It's like a radar, but for gay people.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Where do you buy it?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Sharper Image. It's right next to the USB-powered Popsicle maker.

jokes.text: It's sold out. Yeah, sorry about that. That's a bummer. Well, they're sold out. Damn. Try Brookstone.
jokes.setup: Michael's gaydar device lie
jokes.punchline: Escalating the lie with multiple store checks
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Michael commits to the bit by checking multiple stores, making it more absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I have a gaydar. It's extremely accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's not a real thing.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yes it is. I went to RadioShack and I got it.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: RadioShack doesn't sell gaydar devices.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't find it at RadioShack. I found it at Best Buy.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Best Buy doesn't have those either.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Actually, I got it at the Sharper Image. They have all kinds of crazy stuff there.

jokes.text: Yeah, I didn't go through with the wedding. I got cold feet a few days before, and... I can't really explain it. I just had to get out of that relationship.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining her canceled wedding
jokes.punchline: Understating the Jim situation as just 'getting out'
jokes.explanation: Subtle humor in Pam's understatement, more touching than funny but has comedic awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim and I were supposed to get married, but he got out.

jokes.text: and I have lunch for the next five weeks.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining the wedding cancellation aftermath
jokes.punchline: The practical upside of having frozen wedding food
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery - finding the silver lining in a failed wedding through frozen food logistics.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Well, the practical upside is we have enough frozen wedding food to last us like six months.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's not really a silver lining, Pam.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It kind of is though. Do you know how expensive catering is?

jokes.text: I got them a toaster. They called off the wedding and gave the toaster back to me. I tried to return the toaster to the store, and they said they no longer sold that kind of toaster. So now my house has got two toasters.
jokes.setup: Kevin explaining his wedding gift situation
jokes.punchline: The accumulation of toasters through bureaucratic incompetence
jokes.explanation: Good escalation comedy - the mundane problem building to an absurd conclusion.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: I got married three times. Three different toasters. Never bought one.

jokes.text: It is so cool that you're gay. I totally underestimated you.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to be supportive of Oscar
jokes.punchline: Implying being gay makes Oscar cooler than expected
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael back-handed compliment - trying to be supportive while being offensive.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oscar, I just want you to know that I'm very supportive of your lifestyle. And I have to say, I'm impressed. I did not know you were cool.

jokes.text: Yes, I am super cool. I am an accountant at a failing paper supply company. In Scranton. Much like, uh, Sir Ian McKellen.
jokes.setup: Michael calling Oscar cool for being gay
jokes.punchline: Oscar's sarcastic comparison of his mundane life to a famous gay actor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant sarcasm - the Ian McKellen comparison perfectly skewers Michael's shallow gay stereotyping while highlighting Oscar's ordinary life.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Yeah, Michael, I'm just like Rock Hudson. Except instead of being a famous actor, I'm a accountant in Scranton, Pennsylvania. And instead of being closeted, I'm just really, really boring.

jokes.text: Sure, sometimes I watch 'Will & Grace.' And I want to throw up. It's terribly loud. I do like it sometimes, when Harry Connick Jr. is on. He's so talented.
jokes.setup: Oscar discussing gay stereotypes in media
jokes.punchline: His specific critique ending with genuine appreciation for Harry Connick Jr.
jokes.explanation: Good character development - Oscar rejecting gay stereotypes while admitting to one specific appreciation.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: You know, gay men are always portrayed as these flamboyant stereotypes in the media. It's frustrating because we're so much more diverse than that. But you know who I respect? Harry Connick Jr. That man can sing, act, and he's genuinely talented. Not a stereotype in sight.

jokes.text: Okay. Who put my calculator in Jell-O?
jokes.setup: Dwight discovering his calculator in Jell-O
jokes.punchline: The visual of the encased calculator and his measured reaction
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim prank format - the visual gag of office supplies in Jell-O is a series staple.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My calculator! Idiots!

jokes.text: I need to know who put my calculator in Jell-O or I'm gonna lose... my freakin' mind!
jokes.setup: Dwight's measured initial reaction to the prank
jokes.punchline: His building frustration to near-explosion
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Dwight's controlled facade breaking down over a simple prank.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: What is this? What am I looking at here?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This is... this is unacceptable.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Who did this? I demand to know who is responsible for this.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This is not funny. This is sabotage. This is a direct attack on my workspace, my productivity, and my dignity as Assistant Regional Manager.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I will find out who did this, and there will be consequences. Severe. Consequences.

jokes.text: You know, it-it's amazing to me that in this day and age, you could be so... obtuse... a-about sexual orientation! I watch 'The L Word,' Okay? I watch 'Queer as [beep].' That's not what it's called.
jokes.setup: Michael defending his progressive attitudes
jokes.punchline: Getting the show title wrong while claiming expertise
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael - undermining his own argument through ignorance while trying to sound informed.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm very progressive. I watch a lot of TV. I'm familiar with all the major shows.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I love 'Will & Grace.' I love 'Friends.' I love 'Sex and the City.'

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Those are all shows about gay people.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yes, and I'm very supportive of the gays. I watch 'Queer... Eye for the Straight Guy.'

jokes.text: Well, gay pride, right? Gay pride parade? It's not like... Gay Shame Festival.
jokes.setup: Michael defending gay pride concept
jokes.punchline: Inventing 'Gay Shame Festival' as the opposite
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic - 'Gay Shame Festival' is perfectly Michael's backwards reasoning made funny.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm all for gay pride. It's a beautiful thing. But you know what the opposite would be? Gay Shame Festival. Where we just walk around and point at people like, 'Shame! Shame on you for being gay!' No, that's not... that's not a good idea.

jokes.text: Could Oscar and Angela be having a gay affair? No. Maybe. Is that what this is about? No. I don't. No. It's not possible. Anything's possible.
jokes.setup: Michael speculating about office relationships
jokes.punchline: His rapid contradictory statements about Oscar and Angela
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Michael's scattered thinking and inability to process information logically.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oscar and Angela? No, no, no. They would never... I mean, Oscar's... and Angela's so... No.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? Yeah. Yeah, I could see that. They're both very... organized.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, wait. No. That's crazy. Forget I said anything.

jokes.text: Well, I'm not gay, Jan, and you should know that better than anybody.
jokes.setup: Jan asking Michael to imagine if he were gay
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate reference to their relationship
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriateness - making their personal relationship part of a professional discussion.

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Just imagine if you were gay.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: If I were gay, I would think you were a woman.

jokes.text: The company has made it my responsibility today to put an end to 100,000 years of being weirded out by gays.
jokes.setup: Michael taking responsibility for sensitivity training
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous timeframe of '100,000 years'
jokes.explanation: Great Michael grandiosity - making himself responsible for all of human evolution and prejudice.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I will personally ensure that this office is a beacon of sensitivity and acceptance. I'm committing to 100,000 years of diversity training.

jokes.text: Am I the first gay man you ever knew? Trick question. Because you can't always tell, so how would I know? Was that the right answer?
jokes.setup: Michael asking Oscar a question about being the first gay person he knew
jokes.punchline: His convoluted logic making it a 'trick question'
jokes.explanation: Good Michael logic - turning a simple question into an overcomplicated puzzle then seeking validation.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: So Oscar, were you the first gay person I ever met?

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: I don't know, Michael. Probably.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: See, that's a trick question though, because if you were the first gay person I knew, then I didn't know you were gay until I knew you. So technically, you weren't gay until I knew about it.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: That's not how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, from my perspective, you became gay.

jokes.text: You know what? Gay porn, straight porn, it's all good. I don't particularly get into this, but, uh... You know what? I totally see the merit. And, actually... It is quite beautiful.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to show he's not homophobic while looking at gay porn
jokes.punchline: His increasingly uncomfortable over-compensation about finding it 'beautiful'
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael overcompensation - his discomfort visible through excessive praise.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: This is actually beautiful. The cinematography, the lighting... it's really well done.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I mean, look at the way they're... connected. It's very artistic, very tasteful.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, I appreciate all forms of human expression. This is no different than a Michelangelo. Two bodies, entwined... it's poetry.

jokes.text: Did you know that 'gay' used to mean 'happy'? When I was growing up, it meant 'lame.' And now it means a man who makes love to other men.
jokes.setup: Michael giving a linguistic history lesson
jokes.punchline: His crude and clinical definition of current usage
jokes.explanation: Good Michael educational moment gone wrong - his awkward clinical terminology undermines his attempt at sophistication.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: The word 'gay' has had a very interesting history. It originally meant 'happy' or 'carefree.' Then in the mid-20th century, it became a term for homosexual individuals. And now, in 2010, it just means 'bad.'

jokes.text: We're all homos. Homo sapiens.
jokes.setup: Michael's diversity training speech
jokes.punchline: His obvious and inappropriate pun on 'homo'
jokes.explanation: Lazy pun but perfectly Michael - his proud delivery of an obvious joke makes it work character-wise.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We need to create a workplace where everyone feels welcome, regardless of their sexual orientation. Whether you're heterosexual or homo... sapiens.

jokes.text: Oscar, why don't you take this opportunity to officially come out to everybody here, however you want to do it. Go ahead. Stand up. I'm doing this for you.
jokes.setup: Michael running sensitivity training
jokes.punchline: Forcing Oscar to come out publicly while claiming it's for Oscar's benefit
jokes.explanation: Peak Michael selfishness disguised as altruism - forcing someone's most personal moment for his own comfort.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oscar, I think it's time you came out. For you. For your own good.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Michael, I'm already out.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, I mean really out. Publicly. Everyone should know. It's nothing to be ashamed of.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: I've told you, everyone here already knows.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well then you should tell them again. Louder. So they really hear it.

jokes.text: I am not offended by homosexuality. In the '60s, I made love to many, many women. Often outdoors. In the mud and the rain. And it's possible a man slipped in. There would be no way of knowing.
jokes.setup: Creed sharing his thoughts on homosexuality
jokes.punchline: His bizarre story about potentially accidental gay sex
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant Creed moment - completely unexpected, perfectly in character, and hilariously specific. The mud/rain detail elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I'm not a homophobe. I'm cool with gay people. I have a very open mind. Back in the '60s, I experimented. I was in a band, you know, there were a lot of drugs going around. One time, I ended up in a car with this guy, and we did some things I'm not proud of. But I didn't know he was a guy until halfway through.

jokes.text: I bet a lot of straight men wished that applied to them. So they could go out there and have some torrid, unabashed, monkey sex.
jokes.setup: Michael discussing gay marriage laws
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate characterization of gay relationships as 'monkey sex'
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael crudeness masked as progressive thinking - his inappropriate language undermines his intended message.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not homophobic. I'm all for gay marriage. But calling it 'monkey sex'? That's just what I call it.

jokes.text: I think all the other office gays should identify themselves, or I will do it for them. No one else in this office is gay. What about Phyllis?
jokes.setup: Dwight wanting to identify other gay employees
jokes.punchline: Immediately suggesting Phyllis despite Michael's denial
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight moment - his authoritarian approach to identifying gay people is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: So which employees do you think are gay?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't know, Dwight. Why are you asking me this?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, what about Phyllis?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Phyllis? No, no, Phyllis is not gay.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Are you sure? She has very short hair.

jokes.text: And frankly, kind of amazing. See? Everybody has a chance.
jokes.setup: Phyllis announcing her engagement to Bob Vance
jokes.punchline: Michael's back-handed compliment implying her engagement is surprising
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael back-handed compliment - turning congratulations into an insult with complete obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wow, Phyllis. Bob Vance. I have to say, I am very surprised. And happy for you. But mostly surprised.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Bob Vance, Vance Refrigeration? He's really stepped up.

jokes.text: Of course, we all thought you were gay in high school. Yeah, right. Ha, ha! And I take that as a compliment.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Phyllis potentially being gay
jokes.punchline: Phyllis revealing everyone thought Michael was gay in high school
jokes.explanation: Excellent reversal - Phyllis turning the tables on Michael with a perfectly delivered burn that he tries to play off.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Everyone thought you were gay in high school.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What? No!

jokes.text: I would be the most flamboyant gay you've ever seen. I would be leading the parade covered in feathers, and just... I'd be waving that rainbow flag.
jokes.setup: Michael defending his heterosexuality
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top description of hypothetical gay behavior
jokes.explanation: Good Michael overcompensation - his detailed fantasy of being flamboyantly gay reveals his stereotypical thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not gay. I'm very much into women. But if I were gay, I'd be an amazing gay guy. I'd probably... I don't know, wear really cool clothes and go to the best restaurants and have tons of friends who thought I was hilarious.

jokes.text: You're ignorant. And insulting. And small.
jokes.setup: Michael forcing unwanted physical contact
jokes.punchline: Oscar's perfectly delivered three-word condemnation
jokes.explanation: Excellent Oscar moment - the escalating adjectives perfectly capture his frustration with surgical precision.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Personal space.

jokes.text: You know what? I'm gonna raise the stakes. I want you to watch this. And I want you to burn this into your brains. Because this is an image that I want you people to remember. For a long time to come.
jokes.setup: Michael preparing for some grand gesture
jokes.punchline: His dramatic buildup to what's presumably going to be a kiss
jokes.explanation: Great buildup - Michael's grandiose announcement makes whatever's coming both more anticipated and more cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I have something very important to tell you. Something I've been waiting to say for a long time.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm going to lean in now, and when I do, I want you to know that this means everything to me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Here it comes...

jokes.text: I did it.
jokes.setup: Michael's dramatic buildup to kissing Oscar
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact declaration after the kiss
jokes.explanation: Perfect anticlimax - two words that perfectly capture Michael's self-congratulatory attitude after crossing a major boundary.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm going to kiss Oscar.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: What? No!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not gay.

jokes.text: What is love, anyway? Maybe it's supposed to break all the rules. Like me and Jan. Or Oscar and some guy.
jokes.setup: Michael philosophizing about love
jokes.punchline: Equating his relationship with Jan to Oscar's hypothetical relationship
jokes.explanation: Good Michael moment - making everything about himself while trying to be profound and inclusive.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, love is like a fart. If you have to force it, it's probably crap.

jokes.text: That's what she said. Or he said.
jokes.setup: Michael saying they 'put this matter to bed'
jokes.punchline: His signature catchphrase modified for inclusivity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback modification - updating his signature joke for gay inclusivity is perfectly Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We're gonna put this matter to bed.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said. Or he said. Or they said. I'm not gonna assume.

jokes.text: Kids, sometimes it pays to be gay.
jokes.setup: Oscar explaining his three-month paid vacation settlement
jokes.punchline: His sardonic life lesson about leveraging discrimination
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - Oscar finding the silver lining in discrimination with perfect deadpan delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: I got three months paid vacation out of the company. You know what I learned? If you're gonna be discriminated against, you might as well get paid for it.

jokes.text: It works. Heh.
jokes.setup: Jim setting up some kind of prank or device
jokes.punchline: His satisfied reaction to whatever just worked
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim prank satisfaction - his quiet joy in a successful scheme.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: His satisfied reaction to whatever just worked

tmdb_overview: When Michael unintentionally outs a gay employee, he stumbles through a sensitivity mine-field. Also, we find out what happened with Jim and Pam.
insight: Season premiere pivots hard toward cringe with 2.95 jokes per minute, 81.1 impact.
